Betting as a thin river bluff is high-variance against an UTG checking range that often contains Kx/Qx or showdown-capable hands that won’t fold to a single bluff, and the pot is small (750). I’ll check to realize my (essentially zero) equity and avoid bloating the pot with air; if Elon bets I’ll fold to meaningful pressure and only entertain calling a very small probe with a specific read.

UTG in a 4-handed game with 9♦6♥ offsuit is too weak to open. This hand performs poorly out of position, is often dominated, and limp-calling or min-raising invites multiway pots or re-raises from aggressive opponents (Flash and Elon). With ~56 BB I don't need to gamble from UTG early in the tournament — preserve chips and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with 9-4 offsuit. This is a very weak starting hand that does not play well post-flop, even in a four-handed game. While the button is a prime stealing position, 94o is far too low in my opening range to justify a raise, and there is no reason to limp. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Excellent opportunity to apply pressure with premium hand. I hold Js Qs, a strong broadway hand in the top 10% of starting hands. Minni and Flash have folded, leaving me heads-up against Elon in the big blind who has only 4,900 chips remaining. I'm in excellent position to apply maximum pressure.

Cost to call is minimal (50 chips), but I should be aggressive here given: 1) hand strength, 2) favorable position (SB), 3) Elon's short stack makes him desperate, and 4) I have the chip advantage. A raise to 300 applies solid pressure without overcommitting - it's roughly 2x the minimum raise and will force Elon to make a decision with limited chips. This is a strong value-building spot that maximizes my edge.

Preflop in the big blind facing a raise to 300 from the small blind Claude after two folds. My hand is T2 offsuit, a very weak holding with poor playability, low equity against any reasonable raising range from SB, and no position postflop. Pot odds are about 3:1 to call 200 more into 400, but the hand is too weak to defend profitably given the likelihood of being dominated or outdrawn. Folding preserves my stack for better spots in this deep-stack tournament phase. Standard fold with trash.

I am holding Qs 2d in the UTG position. This is a very weak hand that does not play well post-flop, and opening with it from early position is not profitable. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I'm on the button with 7d-Qc (weak off-suit queen). Flash folded UTG, and I'm facing a 100 chip call into a 150 chip pot. While I do have position and reasonable pot odds (3:1), Q7o is simply too weak to play profitably from any position. The hand ranks in the bottom 20% of all starting hands and will frequently be dominated or out of position postflop against two opponents. Given that I'm building a stack over 150 hands, I need to be selective with my hand selection. With only 4 players and the blinds already posting (Elon 50/Minni 100), I should wait for premium hands or at least better speculative hands on the button. This is a straightforward fold.

Preflop, folded to me in the small blind with A9o, a strong hand with an ace and good playability. Positionally challenging postflop, but raising steals the dead money from blinds and builds the pot when ahead of BB calling ranges. Minni in BB has 55BB stack and from history shows willingness to defend but often checks/folds weak boards. Standard play is to raise 3x BB (300 total) for value and initiative. Equity advantage vs typical BB defense (~60%+ vs calling range). Avoid limping OOP which invites multiway pots or raises.

Facing Elon's 3x open to 300 from the small blind, I'm in the big blind with 9♠8♥ and ~55BB effective. This is a defendable, playable hand heads-up: it has straight potential and reasonable equity vs a likely wide SB opening range. I don't want to 3-bet out of position with an offsuit, speculative hand (it bloats the pot and invites 4-bets), so I’ll call the extra 200 to see a flop and proceed based on texture — play aggressively if I connect, otherwise fold to heavy post‑flop pressure.

Flop of Ts Ad 7c gives me top pair top kicker (Ah pairs the Ad with 9 kicker) on a somewhat coordinated board with possible straight draws (e.g., JT, 89) and a club flush draw. I raised preflop from SB and got called by BB Minni, who has a wide defending range including Ax weaker, sets, two-pair, draws. OOP, betting for value against her worse Ax, pairs, draws, while protecting against floats or draws. Pot is 600; a 2/3 pot bet of 400 builds the pot, denies equity to draws, and fits my strong range. Minni's history shows she calls but folds to continued aggression often. Checking risks giving free cards or facing her bets with weaker hands.

Preflop I defended the BB with 9♠8♥ as planned. On the T♠ A♦ 7♣ flop Elon leads for 400 into a ~600 pot. My hand is a strong drawing hand here: I have a double‑gutshot (J or 6 completes the straight) = 8 outs. That gives me ~17% to hit the turn and ~31.5% to hit by the river. Calling 400 to win a 1,400 pot after my call (~3.5:1) requires ~22% equity, so my draw is +EV to simply call. Raising OOP is an option, but it bloats the pot with only a draw, gives him easy ways to continue with top pair/Ax, and may induce a shove from his stack size — I prefer to realize equity rather than commit more now. Plan: call the flop, evaluate the turn — if I hit (6/J) or pick up additional equity I'll play aggressively; if I miss and face heavy turn pressure I'll fold. Therefore I will call the 400.

Turn board Ts Ad 7c 2h. I have Ah9c for top pair top kicker, blocking AA/AK/sets with Aces. Preflop raiser OOP, c-bet flop called by Minni's wide BB defending range (worse Ax like AQo-ATo, mid pairs 88-99, sets unlikely, draws like J9s/89s flush/straight draws). Turn 2h is a low brick, adding weak heart draw (but I block nuts with Ah) without completing strong draws; doesn't change my equity much (~75%+ vs her range). Pot 1400, first to act: betting ~2/3 pot (900) extracts value from her weaker Ax/pairs/draws that call flop, protects vs any equity (e.g., remaining clubs/hearts), maintains initiative, and represents strength. Her history shows calling stations tendencies but folds to aggression (e.g., Hand 11, 21); stacks deep enough (42BB me, 50BB her) to bet without overcommitting. Checking risks free card to draws/improves or her check-raise bluff/value, pot control suboptimal with TPTK OOP vs loose defender. Value bet sizing denies equity while building pot for river.

Fold. On the turn I have 9♠8♥ on T♠ A♦ 7♣ 2♥ and Elon has bet 900 into a 2300 pot. I have 8 clean outs (6s or Js) → 8/46 ≈ 17.4% to hit the river. To call 900 and win the current pot I need ~28.1% equity (900 / (2300+900)), so a pure call is -EV. Raising OOP as a semi‑bluff is possible, but Elon is aggressive and likely to call or shove, which would force me to commit a big chunk of my ~49.5BB stack with only 17% to improve. With poor pot odds, limited implied odds and being out of position, the correct play is to fold and preserve my stack for better spots.

Folding Q3o from UTG. This is a clear fold decision for several reasons:

1. **Hand Quality**: Q3 offsuit is one of the worst hands in poker - weak top card with a terrible kicker that's easily dominated.

2. **Position**: UTG is the earliest position with 3 opponents still to act. Playing marginal hands from early position is a fundamental mistake that bleeds chips over time.

3. **No Stack Pressure**: With 13,900 chips (139 BBs), I'm in a healthy position and don't need to play weak hands. I can afford to wait for premium holdings.

4. **Pot Odds Insufficient**: Even at 2.5:1 pot odds to call, Q3o lacks the equity to be profitable. The hand performs poorly against the likely range of raises from the blinds.

5. **Table Image**: I've been playing relatively tight through 33 hands. Maintaining that disciplined image is valuable for when I do enter pots with strong hands.

This is a standard fold from UTG with a marginal hand.

Kd5h is a marginal offsuit hand on the button with no prior action except UTG fold. It plays poorly multiway postflop, gets dominated by better kings, and lacks strong equity or playability deepstacked. SB and BB will defend wide, leading to tough spots. Standard fold to preserve stack for premium spots in this tournament phase.

Heads-up in the small blind vs the big blind. Pot is 150 and I need to add 50 to complete; my hand is Qc5d — marginal offsuit queen but perfectly playable heads-up. In a heads-up SB spot I should be aggressive and raise a wide range to avoid playing OOP and to generate fold equity. Raising to the min (200 total) is the standard, low-variance play: it adds pressure, isolates Flash, and keeps pot size manageable. If Flash 3-bets large I will fold this marginal holding given our ~49BB stack; if he calls I’ll continuation-bet favorable textures and play straightforwardly postflop. Commit 200 total (I add 150 to the 50 already posted).

In a blind-vs-blind situation, I am facing a min-raise from Minni in the Small Blind. I only need to call 100 more into a pot of 300, giving me 3:1 pot odds. King-high (K5o) has sufficient equity against a wide Small Blind opening range to justify a call and see a flop in position.

I opened preflop to 200 and Flash called; flop is 4h 8c 8h and I hold Qc5d — no pair, no hearts, very little showdown value. As the preflop aggressor I should continue to apply pressure and try to take down the pot now rather than giving Flash a free card. A half-pot c-bet (200 into 400) gives good fold equity versus his wide BB calling range while keeping the pot controllable if I’m called. Plan: bet to take it down; if Flash raises strongly I’ll fold this marginal hand; if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn and only barrel with equity or a realistic fold target.

In this blind-vs-blind situation, the flop is 4h-8c-8h. Minni leads for 200 into a 400 pot. I am getting 3:1 pot odds to call. My hand is King-high, which holds some showdown value against a wide small-blind range that often consists of air or lower high-card combinations. Given the paired board and the fact that I have position, I will call once to see the turn and evaluate. If Minni continues with aggression on the turn, I will likely fold unless I improve.

Check. I have Q♣5♦ with no pair and no meaningful backdoors; the Ace on the turn is a scare card that helps a large portion of the range that called my flop c‑bet (A‑x, A8, 8x, 4x and other showdown/float hands). My fold equity on a second barrel is low and Flash is deep/sticky enough (has shown willingness to call down) that a turn bluff is unlikely to succeed. Pot control is the priority — the pot is 800 and my stack (~4,550) makes committing chips with no equity a losing line. Plan: check and see his action; if he bets I will fold to any sizable value bet (only consider calling a very tiny probe), if he checks back I keep the pot small and re-evaluate the river for a possible small bluff or showdown attempt.

On the turn, an Ace appears, which is a card that generally favors the pre-flop raiser's range. However, Minni has checked. My current hand is King-high, which has very little showdown value but could potentially win against a total bluff. By checking back, I see a free river and avoid inflating the pot with a weak hand. If Minni is checking a made hand (like an 8 or an Ace) to trap, checking back is the safest play. If she is giving up, I might win at showdown or have a better opportunity to bluff on the river if the situation warrants it.

I hold Q♣5♦ on 4♥ 8♣ 8♥ A♦ K♠. I c‑bet the flop and was called; both players checked the turn. I have no pair, no meaningful blocker to the parts of Flash's calling range, and almost no showdown value. Flash’s flop‑call/turn‑check range likely contains many hands that beat me (A‑x, K‑x, 8x, 4x, pocket pairs), and he’s demonstrated a tendency to call down. Betting the river to bluff would require him to fold frequently — unlikely given his tendencies — and would risk a large portion of my ~4550 stack to win an 800 pot. Pot control is the +EV line: check and fold to any meaningful bet rather than bluffing here.

On the river, I have improved to a pair of Kings. The board is 4h-8c-8h-Ad-Ks. Minni led the flop but checked both the turn (Ace) and the river (King). Her checks suggest she likely lacks an Ace or an 8. While my pair of Kings is a strong hand in this sequence, betting might only get called by hands that beat me (like a slow-played Ace or a better King) and would likely force folds from hands I already beat. Checking back allows me to realize my showdown value and win the pot against her missed draws or small pocket pairs.

UTG with J6s in a 4-handed game at 50/100 blinds. J6 suited is a speculative hand with poor playability and dominated often. Position is the worst pre-flop, so folding avoids tough post-flop spots OOP with a weak hand. Stacks are deep (56BB effective), but no need to play marginal hands from early position early in the tournament. Preserve stack for better spots.

On the button with 4♣9♥ — a weak, unsuited hand with very little multiway equity — but excellent positional opportunity to steal the blinds. Pot is 150, it costs 100 to just call (which is a bad limp into two deep/aggressive blinds). I want to apply pressure and take the pot now, so I’ll open to a standard 3x BB sizing (300) to maximize fold equity while keeping postflop decisions manageable. Plan: if I get a lot of resistance (a significant 3-bet) I’ll fold this holding; if called I’ll proceed cautiously on the flop (c-bet only with real equity or favorable texture, otherwise check/fold to heavy pressure).

85s is a marginal suited gapper. While it has some playability, defending from the Small Blind against a 3x open from an active Button player is generally unprofitable in the long run due to being out of position for the remainder of the hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

K8o in the big blind versus a button raise. Minni has been aggressive from the button with a shorter stack (4,250 chips). I'm getting 2.25:1 pot odds (450 to call 200), which means I need ~31% equity to break even. K8o has reasonable playability and should have ~45% equity against Minni's likely button raising range. Key factors: (1) Excellent pot odds with two cards to come, (2) Position postflop - I can check and react to Minni's actions, (3) Minni's wider button range gives me decent equity, (4) The stack-to-pot ratio is healthy for postflop play. Defending the big blind here is standard poker, and the pot odds justify a call with this holding.

On the flop with K8o (K-high), I have a marginal holding on a board of 7d-9d-2c. While I got decent pot odds preflop to defend the big blind, this flop doesn't connect well with my hand. I have no pair, no draw, just king-high. 

Key considerations:
1. Out of position: I'll need to act first on every street, giving Minni positional advantage
2. Minni's aggressive profile: She raised from the button with 4,250 chips - a shorter stack that might be playing more hands
3. Stack dynamics: Both players remaining are relatively healthy; folding equity is less valuable
4. Hand strength: K8o is margininal here and likely behind most of Minni's raising range that connects with this board

The best approach is to check and let the action develop. If Minni c-bets (which is likely), I can reassess based on pot odds and her bet size. This allows me to:
- Control pot size with a marginal hand
- Keep the option to fold if facing significant aggression
- Potentially improve on the turn (any K or 8 gives me a pair)
- Get more information about her hand strength

Checking is standard defensive play here.

I have top pair (9♥) with a weak 4-kicker, heads-up and in position on a two-diamond flop. Claude's calling range from the BB includes many draws and worse pairs, so I want to extract value and charge diamond draws while keeping the pot controllable. I'll c-bet ~half-pot to get value/deny equity; if he check-raises large I'll fold and re-evaluate if he simply calls.

K8o on 7d-9d-2c is a marginal holding facing aggression from Minni's button raise. While the pot odds appear reasonable (3.86:1, needing ~20.6% equity), my actual equity against Minni's flop betting range is likely only 15-25%. I have no made hand, no draws, just king-high with limited playability. Critical factors: (1) Out of position - I'll be in tough spots on subsequent streets, (2) Minni's profile suggests aggression both with value and air, but I need better than K-high to continue, (3) My stack is healthy (13,600) - no need to gamble with marginal holdings, (4) Folding equity is limited here due to stack sizes, (5) Any continuation will likely result in more pressure on the turn. This is a disciplined fold of a weak unpaired hand out of position.

UTG with J♠7♥ — a weak, offsuit jack in first-to-act position. With 49BB effective and only the blinds in the pot, my opening range should be much stronger (broadways, medium+ pairs, suited connectors). Calling 100 out of position is a passive, low-EV line and a min-raise to 200 is thin and likely to be 3-bet or dominated postflop. Better to preserve chips and wait for a cleaner spot or position. Fold.

I am on the button with 6h 3c, which is a very weak starting hand. Minni has already folded. While I am in a good position to steal, 63 offsuit is too weak to justify a raise or a call against the remaining players, especially since my goal is to maintain my chip lead and wait for better opportunities.

I'm in the small blind with 7-2 offsuit facing a heads-up situation against Elon's big blind. While 7-2 is traditionally the worst hand in poker, several factors favor calling here: (1) I only need to call 50 additional chips, which represents excellent pot odds of 3:1 (150 to call 50); (2) With these odds, I need only ~25% equity to break even, and 7-2 likely has close to that against a random BB range in heads-up play; (3) Elon is critically short-stacked with only 5,500 chips, giving him limited flexibility; (4) Heads-up play features much wider calling/raising ranges, and 7-2 has reasonable play in such dynamics; (5) The 50-chip investment is minimal relative to my 13,550 stack and the tournament structure (115 hands remaining). Folding would be overly tight given the immediate pot odds and short-handed context.

Preflop in the big blind facing a small blind limp after folds from UTG and Button. Pot is 200 with no money to call. My hand Jd2c is very weak offsuit with poor connectivity and playability postflop. Raising light is possible heads-up vs SB limp (which is wide), but J2o lacks showdown value, blockers, or strong equity realization even in position. Best to check and realize equity cheaply on the flop where I act last, controlling the pot with a speculative hand. Avoid bloating the pot OOP for opponent preflop.

On the flop Th 7d Qh with 7c 2d, I've hit a pair of sevens, which is a significant hand in this heads-up scenario. While 7-2 is traditionally weak, the pair gives me showdown value against Elon's range in the big blind. Key factors supporting a bet: (1) I have a made hand (pair of 7s) that's likely ahead of many of Elon's holdings; (2) The flop is relatively dry without multiple draw possibilities; (3) Elon is critically short-stacked (5,500 chips), limiting his flexibility and fold equity; (4) A bet of 125 (62.5% of the 200-chip pot) is standard sizing that extracts value from weaker holdings while maintaining pot control; (5) Acting first out of position, I should take the initiative to define the hand strength. This sizing gives Elon reasonable odds to call with marginal holdings, which is favorable given my pair. If called, I'll reassess on the turn based on cards that hit.

Heads-up on a Th7dQh flop after checking BB to SB limp. My Jd2c is jack-high with no pair, no straight draw (jack doesn't connect well), and no flush draw. Pure air. Claude's small donk bet (125 into 325) suggests either a weak made hand, draw, or probe, but my hand has negligible equity (~20-30% at best vs any reasonable range) and poor playability. No reason to continue; folding preserves stack for better spots. SB limps are wide historically, but betting this board filters out total trash. Easy fold.

In a 4-handed game, Ace-Eight offsuit (A8o) is a strong enough hand to open from the Under The Gun position. As the chip leader, I want to maintain aggression and put pressure on the shorter stacks (Elon and Minni). A standard 3x big blind raise to 300 is appropriate to take control of the pot pre-flop.

With 7-5 offsuit on the button against Flash's UTG raise to 300, I should fold. This is one of the weakest hands in poker (7-5o), and Flash's early position raise indicates a stronger holding. While position on the button is valuable, it's not valuable enough to justify playing marginal hands like 7-5o postflop. The risk-reward ratio is unfavorable - I would be calling 300 chips with a hand that connects poorly to most flops and puts me in difficult postflop spots. With a healthy 13,700 chip stack (91 big blinds), there's no need to take marginal spots. I should preserve chips for premium hands and better situations.

Preflop in the small blind facing an UTG raise to 300 from Flash, who has been aggressive but UTG raises typically indicate strength. My hand is 2h5c, a complete trash hand with no high card value, poor connectivity, and offsuit. Pot odds are calling 250 into 450, but out of position postflop with this hand offers no playability, minimal fold equity on a raise, and the BB can still act. Standard fold to avoid spew.

Flash opened to 300 (pot 450). I’m in the BB with Q♠6♥ and need 200 more to call (300 total committed). In this short-handed/heads-up spot UTG/open ranges are wide, so Q6o has reasonable equity heads-up and the call is cheap: 200 into 450 gives ~30.8% break-even equity. 3-betting light OOP with a dominated Q6o is suboptimal and folding is too tight given the odds. I’ll call 200 more (300 total) to see a flop and play cautiously OOP: fold to heavy aggression if I miss, continue with top pair or strong draws, and control pot size when uncertain.

Flop As 4s Ad is very bad for Q♠6♥ — I only have queen-high and a tiny backdoor spade runout. Flash was the preflop raiser and his range is heavy in A‑x and strong broadways that dominate me. I want to control the pot OOP and not bluff into a likely value range, so I’ll keep the pot small and see how he acts. Check and plan to fold to any meaningful bet; only continue if he checks back or gives a clear sign of weakness.

I have flopped trip Aces with an 8 kicker on a board of As-4s-Ad. This is an extremely strong hand. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor and the big blind (Minni) checked to me, a continuation bet is standard. I want to extract value from any pocket pairs, a 4, or spade flush draws. A small bet of roughly 30% of the pot (200 chips) is likely to be called by Minni's defending range while building the pot, whereas a larger bet might scare her off immediately.

Flop: As 4s Ad. I have Qs6h — essentially queen-high with only a tiny backdoor spade runner-runner. Flash was the preflop raiser so his range is heavy in A-x and broadways that dominate me. He bets a small 200 into a pot of 850; calling 200 gives ~19% break‑even equity (200 to win ~1050). My raw equity is marginal against a value-heavy Ace-range but the price is cheap and folding here is too nitty. Raising OOP with nothing is a high-risk bluff with poor fold equity. I’ll call the 200 (500 total committed) to keep the pot small, see a turn, and fold to meaningful turn aggression unless I pick up a clear improvement or a credible bluffing line.

I have Q♠6♥ on As 4s Ad 5h — essentially queen-high with a tiny runner‑runner spade chance. Flash was the preflop raiser and his line (small c-bet on the flop and another small 200 on the turn) is very consistent with A‑x and other value hands that dominate me. Calling 200 into the ~1050 effective pot if I call requires ~19% equity; my raw equity is marginal but the price is cheap and folding every marginal hand here is too nitty. Raising OOP as a bluff is high risk versus an early-position raiser with a big stack, so I’ll keep the pot small and call to see the river. Plan: fold to significant river aggression unless I pair or the river gives a clear bluffing opportunity (villain checks or shows extreme weakness).

I have trip Aces with an 8 kicker on a board of As-4s-Ad-5h. Minni has checked again on the turn. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor and bet the flop, I should continue betting for value. My hand is very strong, only losing to better kickers (A9+), 44, or 55. I want to extract value from any pocket pairs, a 4, a 5, or spade flush draws. A bet of 500 into a 1050 pot is a standard sized value bet that keeps her calling range wide while building the pot for a potential river shove given her remaining stack size.

Board: A♠ 4♠ A♦ 5♥. Pot = 1550, villain (Flash) leads for 500; I (Qs6h) have 4400 behind. Calling 500 into a 2050 total pot requires ~24.4% equity. My realistic outs are only pairing cards (3 queens + 3 sixes = 6 outs) ≈ 13% to hit the river — and many of those pairs still lose to any Ace or a full house. The backdoor spade possibility is dead on the turn. Flash’s line (UTG open, c-bet flop, larger turn bet) is strongly weighted toward A‑x/trips/full houses or other strong value hands, so my fold equity is very low and a bluff raise OOP with Q‑high is high risk and likely to be called. Given the poor pot odds and very slim chance to improve to a winning hand, folding is the +EV play and preserves my stack for a better spot.

3-7 unsuited is statistically one of the weakest hands in poker and should be folded from UTG regardless of stack depth. Being in early position requires a much stronger hand range. With several opponents yet to act and no significant advantage, folding is the mathematically correct play. This preserves chips for stronger opportunities and maintains a disciplined approach to position and hand selection.

On the button with 62s after UTG folds. This is a strong spot to attempt a steal against the blinds or play a suited connector in position with potential for straights and flushes. My stack is 54.5BB deep enough for postflop play. Opponents: Flash in BB is deep and has shown aggression but also calls light; Minni in SB limps frequently. Raising to 300 (3x BB) provides fold equity while keeping pot manageable. Limping is too passive; folding is too tight for this spot.

I’m in the small blind with 5♠9♥ facing a BTN open to 300. This is a weak, poorly playable offsuit hand and I’ll be out of position postflop. Calling 250 into a 450 pot gives only ~26% immediate pot odds, but 5-9o is frequently dominated and has poor equity vs a BTN opening range. There’s also a big, deep BB (Flash) behind who may call or 3-bet, which would put me in uncomfortable multiway/OOP spots. With ~43bb effective I should conserve chips for better spots (pairs, broadways, suited connectors) rather than defend marginally from the SB, so I’ll fold.

I am in the Big Blind holding K4o. Elon has raised to 3x from the Button. While K4o has some high-card value, it is a weak hand to play out of position against a Button raise, especially as I would be calling 200 into a pot of 450. Elon has been relatively active, but I've already accumulated a healthy chip lead (16,350) and don't need to defend with marginal offsuit kings in this spot. I'll fold and wait for a better opportunity to utilize my stack.

Preflop UTG in 4-handed play with QTo offsuit. This hand has decent high-card strength, playability for straights, and blocks broadway. In short-handed tournament poker at 56bb effective depth, UTG open range includes QTo. Limping is suboptimal as it invites multiway pots OOP; folding too tight misses value. Standard small raise sizing of 3x BB observed in prior hands balances range and builds pot profitably against loose calling stations like Claude and Flash. Minni on button may defend wide.

Fold. Elon opened to 300 from UTG (early) which represents a fairly strong range. T♥6♣ is a weak, easily-dominated hand with poor playability postflop — especially against an early-position raiser. Calling 300 into the 450 pot will often make this a multiway pot and reduce any fold equity; 3-betting light (min to 500) is too risky vs an UTG range and could commit too many chips out of position preflop. With ~43.5 BB effective I’ll preserve my stack for a better spot in position or with a real hand.

I have Pocket Queens, which is a premium starting hand. Elon raised to 3x the big blind from under-the-gun. Since I am in the Small Blind and will be out of position for the rest of the hand, I should 3-bet (re-raise) to build the pot and thin the field. A raise to 1,000 (about 3.3x Elon's raise) is a standard sizing to put pressure on the opener and protect my equity.

J8o is too weak to defend in a 3-bet pot out of position. Flash's aggressive 3-bet to 1,000 from the SB indicates strength or light aggression, but either way, I'll be in a difficult postflop situation with a marginal hand. With a healthy 13,600 stack and 111 hands remaining, folding preserves chips for better opportunities and avoids a -EV spot.

Facing a 3-bet from Flash in the SB after my UTG open with QTo offsuit. QTo has decent preflop equity but poor postflop playability OOP, especially offsuit without strong blockers to Flash's 3-bet range (which includes pairs, broadway, suited aces, etc.). Pot odds are ~2:1 (1400:700), but implied odds are weak given position and Flash's aggressive history of continuation betting. 4-betting as a bluff is suboptimal here with this hand at 53bb depth against a deep-stacked opponent who can 5-bet shove or call flat profitably. Folding preserves stack for better spots in 4-handed play where position and hand strength matter more. Prior hands show Flash 3-bets wide but values resistance.

UTG with Js5d is a very weak, unsuited, poorly-connected holding. In 4-handed play ranges are wider, but J5o is still easily dominated by the types of hands the button/SB/BB will continue with (Jx, broadways, pairs, suited connectors). Calling 100 simply to limp in leaves me out of position against multiple players and gives poor post‑flop playability and reverse implied odds. Raising to 200 would be over‑aggressive with a hand that plays badly postflop. Preserve my 43.5bb stack and wait for a better spot — fold.

I am on the button in a 4-handed game with King-Nine offsuit. This is a standard opening range hand from this position to put pressure on the blinds and take control of the pot. Minni has already folded. I will raise to 300, which has been my consistent open-sizing throughout the tournament.

Folding weak offsuit hand (6-10o) from small blind. Out of position against Flash's button raise with only ~35% equity and no strong playability. The positional disadvantage outweighs the pot odds in this spot. Preserving chips to play stronger hands in position is the correct strategy with a reasonable stack.

Preflop in BB facing a button raise to 300 after UTG fold and SB fold. Pot 450, need to call 200 more (total bet 300). Holding Kd8d, suited King with good playability: straight and flush potential, dominates many worse kings. Button ranges are wide and aggressive based on history (Flash frequently raises and continuation bets). Defending wide OOP is standard vs button isolation; pot odds are favorable (~2.25:1). Folding too tight, 3-bet too strong for value/bluff here with this hand. Call to realize equity and see flop cheaply with position dynamics.

Flopped trips with 8d on a board of Ah-8c-8h (paired eights with ace high). Monster hand OOP vs aggressive button raiser (Flash) who frequently c-bets (e.g., Hand 1,5,25). Likely ahead of Ax, KK-QQ, bluffs, draws. Check to disguise strength, induce wide c-bet continuation range, then raise for value/protection. Pot 650, stack 5000 allows multi-street play; betting risks check-back from cautious hands while check exploits opponent's aggression for maximum EV.

The flop is Ah-8c-8h, which is a very strong board for the pre-flop raiser's range. As the aggressor, I have many Aces and pocket pairs in my range that Elon, defending from the big blind, may not have. Since he checked, I will place a standard continuation bet of around 40% of the pot to represent the Ace and take down the pot immediately, as it is unlikely he connected with this board.

Flopped top trips (8s) with Kd kicker on Ah-8c-8h board, an absolute monster disguised after check. Flash's 250 bet into ~650 pot (~38% pot) fits his aggressive c-betting style (frequent in history: Hands 1,3,5,16,22,25,29,37). Wide range: Ax (dominated), overpairs, bluffs, heart flush/straight draws. Check-raise maximizes value from worse hands/draws while protecting against hearts/chases. OOP, building pot now exploits position disadvantage. Raise sizing to 800 (~3.2x his bet, ~90% effective pot) balances value/protection without overcommitting (stack 5000, his 16200 deep). Calling too passive, risks giving free cards/multiway; folding insane.

I opened pre-flop with King-Nine offsuit and Elon defended from the big blind. The flop is Ah-8c-8h. I made a small continuation bet of 250 into 600, and Elon has check-raised me to 800. This check-raise on a paired, Ace-high board usually indicates either a trip Eight, a slow-played Ace, or a complete bluff. Given Elon's relatively short stack (4200 remaining), he might be looking to protect a marginal hand or capitalize on the fact that I might be continuation betting with many air hands. However, I have no piece of this board and only have King-high. Calling a check-raise with no equity and no real draw is a losing play. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

In a 4-handed game, the UTG position is effectively the Cutoff. King-Deuce suited is a marginal hand that sits at the very bottom of a standard opening range or just outside of it. Given that I have a healthy stack and want to avoid playing a weak King out of position or against potentially dominated situations, I will choose to fold this hand and wait for a better spot.

9-4 offsuit is one of the weakest hands in poker. Despite being on the button with good position, this hand has minimal equity and poor playability. Even though I could attempt a blind steal, the risk/reward doesn't justify it—if called, I'm out of position with a weak hand against two opponents who are now short-stacked (Elon 5,850 and Minni 4,250) and may make desperate plays. Folding weak hands from early and middle positions has been part of a disciplined strategy, and this principle applies here. Better spots will come, and preserving chips for premium hands is more important at this stage (hand 41 of 150).

82o is one of the worst possible hands with no connectivity, low cards, and offsuit. Preflop from SB facing no raises, but completing gives postflop position disadvantage with trash equity against BB's wide defending range. Folding preserves stack for better spots; spewing chips early in tournament is -EV. No steal incentive as BB can defend effectively.

Folding 2-9 offsuit from UTG. This is one of the worst hands in poker with no connectivity and poor equity. In early position facing three opponents, there's no profitable play with this hand regardless of stack sizes. Preserving chips for better spots is the correct strategy when tournament position and hand quality are both poor.

9h3s is a complete trash hand: offsuit, gapped, low cards with no potential. Even on the button facing only blinds after UTG fold, this is an easy fold to preserve stack and wait for playable hands. No steal incentive with such weakness against potentially competent opponents. Deep stacks allow patience over 150 hands.

Heads-up from the small blind with 9♠5♦ offsuit is marginal but playable. Facing only 50 to call into a 150 pot gives ~4:1 pot odds (~20% equity required), and versus the big blind's wide range I have reasonable equity to see a flop. I'm out of position and don't want to bloat the pot with a weak, non-connected hand by raising to 200 (would invite 3-bets or larger pots OOP against an aggressive, deep-stacked opponent). I'll call the 50 more (100 total committed) to see a flop and proceed cautiously: continue with pairs/two-pair/draws or fold to heavy pressure on dry boards.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Seven offsuit. Minni completed the small blind, and I have the option to check or raise. Given that my hand is weak and I can see the flop for free, checking is the most logical and conservative play. Raising would likely be met with a call, and I would be playing a bloated pot out of position with a marginal hand.

Heads-up on a K♠ Q♦ 3♥ rainbow flop with 9♠5♦: I have no pair, no real draw (only one spade on board, so no immediate flush draw) and only ~6 outs (~24% to pair by river). Flash checked, showing a wide range of missed overcards, small pairs, or slow-played strong hands. With 4,300 effective and a 200 pot, I want pot control OOP with this marginal hand — checking lets me see a free turn and avoid bloating the pot and facing a raise I have to fold to. Plan: check and fold to any meaningful bet (maybe call a very tiny c-bet only if it’s insignificant), otherwise re-evaluate if I pick up equity on the turn.

I have Jack-high on a board with two broadway cards (King and Queen). I have no pair and no real draws. Minni checked, and since the pot is small and my hand has almost no equity or potential to improve significantly, checking back is the most prudent play. There is no need to bluff into a board that likely connects with many of the cards an opponent might hold when completing the small blind.

Out of position with 9♠5♦ on 3♥ Q♦ K♠ Q♠. I have no pair and no realistic draw (with one spade in hand and only two spades on board I cannot make a flush), only 6 outs to pair on the river (~13% equity). Both players checked the flop, pot is small, and I want to control the size of the pot with a marginal hand. I will check and fold to any meaningful bet (maybe call a very small probe), then only continue on the river if I pair or get a very cheap showdown.

I have Jack-high on a board that contains two Queens and a King. Minni has checked twice. While she may be weak, my hand has virtually no showdown value and very little equity to improve. In a small pot of only 200 chips, there is no compelling reason to bluff, especially since the board texture is more likely to have connected with a variety of hands she could be holding. Checking back allows me to see the river for free.

Board is 3♥ Q♦ K♠ Q♠ 2♦ and I hold 9♠5♦ — no pair, no backdoor, essentially zero showdown value. Flash checked flop and turn, which can be weakness but also a trapping line; given his history he’ll call small river bets often. Bluffing here needs meaningful fold equity (break-even fold% = bet/(pot+bet): half-pot ~33%, pot-sized 50%), and I don’t trust that I can fold him off Kx, Qx or even many overcards. Pot is tiny (200) relative to my 4,300 stack; there are much better spots to risk chips. I’ll preserve my stack and check to showdown rather than firing a marginal river bluff.

I have Jack-high on a board that has double-paired (though one pair is in my hand's range only if I had a Queen, and the other is a King and Queen). Minni has checked through every street after completing from the small blind. While my Jack-high has very little showdown value, the pot is extremely small (200 chips). There is little incentive to bluff here as Minni could easily call with any Ace-high or a small pair like 3s or 2s that she's been trapping or just playing passively. Checking back is the most disciplined play to reach showdown for free in a low-stakes situation.

Pre-flop UTG in a 4-handed game with Jc4s offsuit, a very weak hand with poor playability, no straight or flush potential, and dominated by any raising range. Standard fold to avoid playing out of position with trash. Pot is 150 from blinds (50/100), to call 100 for a limp, but limping weak hands is suboptimal as it invites raises and commits chips poorly. Folding preserves stack for better spots in this tournament aiming to maximize over 150 hands.

Button with K♥Q♦ (strong broadway). Pot is unopened aside from blinds (150). With 4,300 (≈43bb) and two deep blinds behind, I want to take the initiative, build the pot with position, and apply pressure to the blinds. A standard 3xBB open (to 300) is consistent with table tendencies and balances fold equity with not over-committing against potential 3-bets from very deep stacks. Plan: c-bet favorable flops and use position to realize equity; if faced with a large cold 3-bet, re-evaluate (lean fold vs a big 3-bet from these deep players), but proceed postflop against calls.

I am holding 5-3 offsuit in the Small Blind. This is one of the weakest possible starting hands. Minni has raised from the button, and even though I have a large stack, there is no strategic reason to call out of position with such a low-equity hand. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Calling with Tc9s from the big blind. Getting 2.25:1 pot odds (450 to call 200) against Minni's button raise with a short stack (4000 chips). Minni's short stack and button position suggest a wide raising range, giving Tc9s reasonable equity (~40-50%). While out of position post-flop is not ideal, the pot odds and range advantage make this a marginally profitable call. Can play straightforwardly post-flop.

With Tc9s on a 8c 2d Qc flop against Minni's short-stacked button raise, I'm in a marginal situation where checking is the prudent play. I have a gutshot straight draw plus two overcards, giving me reasonable equity (~40-45%) against her wide button-raising range. By checking first to act, I accomplish several things: (1) I gather information about her hand strength, (2) I maintain pot control with a draw rather than a made hand, (3) I allow her to either bet (where I can make a decision based on pot odds) or check (where I can reassess the turn). Her short stack of 4000 chips means she'll likely make a decision soon anyway—this check-and-see approach is more profitable than committing more chips immediately with this drawing hand.

I raised preflop to 300 and Claude (BB) just called. Flop is 8c 2d Qc and Claude checked — I have top pair with the King kicker (K♥Q♦) in position. Heads-up on a mostly dry board except two clubs, this is exactly the spot to c‑bet for value and to deny any backdoor equity. Claude has shown a tendency to call down and seldom check‑raises aggressively, so I want a size that extracts from worse Qx, Kx, 8x and medium pocket pairs while charging any two‑club combos that could be drawing. I’ll make a standard ~60% pot continuation bet to get value and protect my hand but keep the pot manageable with ~40bb effective stack. If Claude raises large I’ll re‑evaluate (likely fold to a very large check‑raise vs sets/turn combos, call smaller raises), but for now bet for value.

With Tc9s on an 8c2dQc flop, I have solid equity: an open-ended straight draw (any J completes the 8-9-T-J-Q straight = 4 outs), plus two overcards to the 8 (3 Ts and 3 9s remaining = 6 more outs). This gives me approximately 40-50% equity against Minni's wide button-raising range. I'm getting 2.625:1 pot odds (1,050 to call 400), which is favorable for a draw with ~40-50% equity. Minni's short stack of 3,600 and button position suggest she's likely pushing with a wide range—many of which are either dominated by my draw or vulnerable to my outs. Calling allows me to: (1) see the turn with good pot odds, (2) gather more information before committing deeper, and (3) leverage her short stack dynamics where she may be forced to play marginal holdings. The check on the flop was correct for information gathering; now that she's shown strength, the call is prudent with this equity and pot odds situation.

Minni checked the turn after showing flop strength, suggesting her hand weakened or she's fearful of the duplicate Queen. With my Tc9s containing the strong Jack-out for an 8-9-T-J-Q straight (plus pair outs), and facing a short stack (~2900), applying pressure is the optimal play. Betting 700 chips creates immediate stack pressure while maintaining strong equity (~40%+) against her likely button-raising range. This forces her into a tough decision while I can still fold to aggression if needed. The turn check indicated weakness - this is the spot to extract value and apply leverage against her diminished resources.

I have K♥Q♦ — trips (Qd) on a paired turn (8c 2d Qc Qh). Pot is 2,150 and Claude has led 700. Claude’s line (call preflop, call my 400 flop c‑bet, lead turn) can include Qx, medium pocket pairs and club draws. Hands that currently beat me (full houses: pocket 22 or pocket 88, or very specific Q+pair combos) exist but are relatively few combos compared to his calling range. Raising here extracts value from worse Qx, Kx and many pocket pairs, charges club draws, and defines my range. I’ll make the minimum raise to 1,400 to keep worse hands and draws in and to get value; if Claude responds with a large 3‑bet or shove I’ll fold (that sizing will usually indicate a full house). If he just calls, I can re‑evaluate the river and press for more value on safe cards.

Calling with Tc9s on 8c-2d-Qc-Qh against Minni's short-stack shove. I have ~10 realistic outs (four Jacks for the straight, plus undercard pairs), giving me 30-40% equity. Pot odds of 6:1 are favorable against this equity. Minni's button range combined with her short-stack dynamics suggests she's pushing with a wide range where I have solid equity. By calling rather than raising, I keep her committed to the pot while preserving the option to stack off if I hit my outs on the river. This is a profitable call with good risk/reward.

With Tc9s on the final board 8c-2d-Qc-Qh-Jd, I've made a Queen-high straight (8-9-T-J-Q), which is a premium hand. This is a significant improvement from my earlier drawing hand. Looking at the action sequence: I raised to 700 on the turn, Minni re-raised to 1400, and I called. Her aggressive turn re-raise suggests strength, likely from hands like QQ, JJ, QJ, or other strong holdings she committed chips with from the button. On the river, with my strong straight and her short stack of ~2200 chips, betting for value is optimal. A bet of 1400 accomplishes several goals: (1) extracts value from her strong made hands like QQ or JJ that are likely to call, (2) applies enough pressure that even marginal holdings (AQ, AT) may fold, and (3) if she shoves, I'm getting excellent pot odds (roughly 3:1) to call with a hand that dominates most of her possible holdings. This bet maximizes expected value against her likely range.

I have trip Queens (Qd + board Qc/Qh) on the river. Claude’s line (call preflop, check‑call flop, lead the turn, call my turn raise, small river bet) represents a lot of Qx and medium pairs — and it can also include the handful of full‑house combos that beat me (pocket 88, pocket 22, Q+8/Q+2 turn full houses and a few river‑made full houses like JJ or QJ). Pot is 5,650 and he bets 1,400; calling 1,400 requires ~20% equity to be +EV. My trips are ahead of most of Claude’s continuing range far more than 20% of the time, so folding would be too nitty. Shoving to 2,200 (my stack) only gains an extra 800 and overcommits me into the relatively small set of full‑house combos that can comfortably call — it’s unnecessary risk. I’ll call the 1,400 to get value from worse Qx, Kx, medium pairs and some bluffs while preserving my remaining stack if I’m up against a monster.

I have 8s9d and only 8 BB (stack 800) in a 50/100 structure. With 8BB the correct framework is push-or-fold — limping or a tiny open to 200 leaves me short with little fold equity and creates awkward postflop decisions. Shoving maximizes fold equity and gives decent equity vs callers; even if called by the big blind or button I have reasonable hands to double up. I will shove my stack.

Minni has raised to 800 (8bb), which appears to be an all-in or a very strong commitment. My hand, 9s 4s, is far too weak to call an 8bb raise, even from the button in a 4-handed game. I also have to consider that Claude and Elon are still to act behind me. Folding is the only viable play here to preserve my stack.

I have Ace-King, one of the strongest hands in poker, in the small blind. Minni has raised to 800 and is all-in (0 chips remaining), and Flash has folded. With AK, I need to apply maximum pressure. A raise to 1500 (minimum raise) accomplishes several goals: (1) It builds the pot with a premium hand, (2) It potentially forces Elon out of the big blind, allowing me to play heads-up or take the pot immediately, and (3) Even if called, I have a very strong hand. AK is equity-positive against virtually any range here, and aggression with premium hands early in the tournament when stack depths are healthy is the correct approach. Folding is out of the question, and merely calling would be too passive given my hand strength and Minni's short stack situation.

Q4 suited is a speculative hand with some flush and straight potential, but it's gapped and offsuit to the queen-high nature. Minni shoved UTG for 800 (all-in), indicating a strong range. Claude 3-bet to 1500 from SB over that, showing even more strength (likely JJ+, AKo/s, etc.). Flash folded. Pot is 2400, calling 1400 commits 25% of my 5750 stack into a pot where I'll be out of position postflop against a strong 3-bet range. Equity calculations: Q4s has poor equity vs tight 3-bet range (~20-30% at best multiway). Pot odds require ~37% equity to call, which we don't have. Folding preserves stack for better opportunities in this deep-stack tournament (44/150 hands). No reason to speculate here.

I'm holding 3d 4h on the button, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands in poker. While position is valuable, 3-4 offsuit has virtually no playability and performs poorly against any opponent's range. With only 100 chips to call, I could justify a call due to pot odds, but folding marginal hands in early tournament phases is strategically sound, especially when better opportunities will arise. I have a healthy stack of 18,000 chips and should preserve capital for premium hands. There's no exploitative value in playing this hand given the stack sizes and my position's already-established advantage.

Preflop in the small blind heads-up against Flash in the big blind with 4d8s, an offsuit trash hand with poor playability, low equity, and no speculative value. Calling the additional 50 into a 150 pot offers minimal fold equity if raised and poor postflop prospects even if flopped well. Folding preserves my 5700 stack (relatively shorter than opponents' deep stacks) for stronger hands in a tournament setting where survival and position matter. Standard fold.

3-handed on the button first to act preflop with blinds posted (pot 150, to call 100). Qh6d is a marginal queen-high offsuit hand, but button stealing range is wide in short-handed play. Raising applies fold equity against blinds who may fold weak holdings, and if called, I have position postflop. Standard raise size around 3x BB. Opponents are deep-stacked and defend often from history, but initiative and position are valuable. Avoid limping to prevent multiway pot OOP for blinds. Folding too tight misses value.

I have KJs in the Small Blind. This is a very strong hand for a three-handed game. Elon on the Button has opened with a 3x raise. With a stack of 5400, Elon is short-stacked compared to Claude and me. I want to build a pot and put pressure on Elon, while also potentially taking control of the hand. Calling is an option, but raising for value is better here to thin the field and isolate the short stack, or take it down pre-flop. I will 3-bet to 1000, which is a standard sizing to provide a tough decision for Elon and put Claude in a difficult spot if he was thinking of defending.

Facing a 3-bet with T9o (Ten-Nine offsuit) is a clear fold. This is a weak, unpaired hand without broadway strength or suited connector potential. Against Flash's 3-bet from the small blind, I'm likely dominated or flipping at best. I'm out of position, and committing 900 chips (5% of my stack) with a marginal hand against two opponents where one has already shown aggression doesn't make sense. With 17,900 chips and a decent position in the tournament at hand 46 of 150, I should preserve my stack and wait for premium holdings or better spots to accumulate chips. Folding is the high-EV decision here.

Facing a 3-bet from the SB after my button open with Qh6d, which is a weak queen-high offsuit hand with poor postflop playability. While I have position heads-up and pot odds of about 2:1 (700 to call into 1400), Q6o is easily dominated by Flash's likely strong 3-bet range (pairs, AK, AQ+, etc.). Calling commits too much stack (13% of my 5400) to a hand unlikely to improve or realize equity effectively OOP initially but with position. 4-betting bluff is possible but risky with this holding against an aggressive player like Flash who has shown strength in big pots. History shows Flash defends and bets aggressively, so folding preserves stack for better spots in 3-handed play. Discipline over marginal defense.

I am on the button with K3o. In a 3-handed game, this is generally a playable hand for a small raise or a call, but given the stack dynamics and the relatively weak kicker, I will simply complete the small blind or call the 100 to see a flop cheaply. However, since the standard play from the button is to either raise or fold, and given my stack size, a min-raise is also viable to pick up the blinds. Looking at previous hands, players are active. I will call the 100 to keep the pot small with a marginal hand.

With 7-6 offsuit from small blind, this is a weak hand that doesn't justify further investment even with 5:1 pot odds. Flash's call suggests a reasonable range, and I'm out of position post-flop with a hand that plays poorly in 3-way pots. While we're early in the tournament (hand 47/150) and stack preservation isn't critical, there's no reason to defend the small blind with such marginal holdings. I should fold and wait for premium opportunities where my stack can be better utilized. The antes/blinds are recoverable, and 7-6o is a clear fold.

Preflop in BB vs button limp after SB folds. Dead money in pot (SB blind). Q6s is a suited, speculative hand with good playability: flush potential, straight draws, dominates many weaker queens or suited hands. Checking realizes equity OOP against button's wide limp range (likely weak offsuit broadways, suited connectors, Ax suited, pairs). Raising risks bloating pot OOP with marginal hand, and button may call wide due to position and deep stacks. Prefer seeing flop cheap with position advantage for opponent but multiway potential low (heads-up likely). Will play postflop based on board texture, leveraging implied odds (53BB stack). Checking balances range, includes dead money.

Flop is 2c-4c-8c, giving me the nut flush draw with Qc6c (9 clean outs to nuts, ~35% equity heads-up). Backdoor straight potential with 6c. Villain Flash limped BTN (wide range: Ax suited clubs, suited connectors, weak broadways, small pairs), which hits this monotone board moderately but often with worse draws (lower clubs like Ac/Jc etc. or non-nut). OOP vs position, but strength warrants a donk bet to build pot, deny equity to villain's draws/floats, extract value from any made hands (88/44/22 unlikely from limp range), and take down dead money frequently. Check risks giving free card to villain's equity or check-back pots. Bet sizing: ~2/3 pot (250 pot) to ~170 gets called by worse, pot-committing when raised. Balances range including top pair/set bluffs pre-check. With 53BB effective, realize fold equity vs deep villain.

On a monotone board of all clubs (2c 4c 8c), I hold Ks 3d, which provides no equity in terms of a flush draw or a made hand. Elon, who was the Big Blind and checked pre-flop, has bet 170 into a 250 pot. Even though the price to call is relatively low, I have no clubs and only overcards that might not be clean outs. Continuing without a club or a pair on this texture is unprofitable in the long run, as I am likely either way behind a made flush, a pair, or a single club draw that has significant equity against me. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Raising with AQ on the button is a fundamental strong poker play. This premium hand merits aggression, especially from the button where I have positional advantage. A 3x BB raise (300 total) is standard sizing to build the pot while keeping the risk/reward favorable. This forces the short-stacked small blind to make a tough decision with 5,500 chips and puts pressure on Flash in the big blind. My position will allow me to react optimally post-flop regardless of which opponent calls.

5c3s is a complete trash hand: low, offsuit, gapped, with minimal playability or showdown value. Facing a button raise to 3x from Claude, who can have a wide range, but in SB we are out of position postflop. Standard SB defense range vs BTN open doesn't include 53o. Calling 250 into 450 pot (getting ~1.8:1) is -EV with this hand's poor equity realization OOP. No squeeze value here as it's not a good bluff candidate. Folding preserves our 55BB stack for premium spots. History shows Claude raises and can have strong hands, but irrelevant for this spot.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Nine offsuit. The Button (Claude) has raised to 300. I need to call 200 more into a pot that will be 650, giving me pot odds of roughly 3.25:1. While Jack-Nine offsuit is a marginal hand to play out of position, it has sufficient equity against a wide Button opening range to justify a call at these odds. I will call to see the flop and play cautiously post-flop.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Nine offsuit on a paired board of 6d-6h-2h. My hand has no connection to the board other than two overcards. Standard play in this scenario as the pre-flop caller is to check to the aggressor (Claude) and see how they react. Leading out (donking) would not represent a strong range here.

I have AQ offsuit on the button with a 650 chip pot. Flash called my preflop raise but checked this dry 6-6-2 flop, indicating weakness or a hand wanting to play a small pot. My AQ is a premium hand that likely has equity advantage over Flash's checking range. The board is uncoordinated and low (66-2), reducing the chance Flash connected strongly. I'm betting approximately 2/3 pot (450) to: (1) apply pressure and take down the pot against missed hands, (2) get value from weaker holdings like KJ or AJ, and (3) maintain my aggressive image. My position allows me to control the action post-flop. This continuation bet is a fundamental play with reasonable hand strength and positional advantage.

I have completely missed the flop with Jack-Nine offsuit on a paired board of 6d-6h-2h. Claude, the pre-flop aggressor, has followed up with a bet of 450 into a 650 pot. While this is a common board for a continuation bet, I have no pieces of the board, no significant draws (aside from very thin backdoors), and I am out of position. I do not have sufficient equity to justify a call, and floating out of position against a capable opponent is generally a losing strategy. Folding is the best choice here.

Preflop on the button with 4s8d offsuit, a weak hand with poor playability, large gaps, and low equity against calling ranges. Pot is 150 with 100 to call, offering poor implied odds given the hand's trash nature. Position is good, but stacks are deep (55BB effective for me), and this hand performs poorly postflop multiway or heads-up against typical calling ranges. Opponents limp/call frequently from history, but raising light risks difficult spots OOP if called. Folding preserves stack for better spots in this tournament phase (hand 49/150). Standard GTO play folds this from button unopened.

I am in the Small Blind with Qd 9s. The action has folded to me. Elon (the Button) folded, meaning I am only facing the Big Blind (Claude). The pot is 150 (my 50 and Claude's 100). It costs me 50 to complete the bet to 100. Qd 9s is a decent hand to play heads-up from the small blind, but raising is generally more effective than just completing. Claude has a large stack (18100), slightly more than mine (16250). Since this is a 3-player situation and I have position post-flop if I raise (though technically the small blind acts first post-flop), I will raise to 300 to try and take the pot down pre-flop or define the hand.

With 6s7h in the big blind facing a small blind raise to 300, I'm holding a marginal weak hand. Flash has shown strength with a positional raise, and I would be out of position post-flop in what is essentially a heads-up situation (Elon folded). My equity against Flash's raising range is likely around 30-35%, which is slightly below the break-even point when considering the 2:1 pot odds. Additionally, I have a healthy stack of 18,100 and can afford to fold weak hands and wait for better spots. There's no value in playing 67o out of position against an aggressive opponent when my stack allows for selective play. Folding preserves chips for stronger situations.

I am on the button with Ts 5s, which is a marginal but playable suited hand. The stack sizes are comfortable, and I am in position. Raising here is a standard play to put pressure on the blinds and potentially win the pot pre-flop. Claude and Elon have shown some aggression, but a standard 3x open (300 total) is consistent with my previous play and allows me to take control of the hand.

K♦8♣ from small blind against Flash's button raise is a marginal hand to play out of position. I'm getting 1.8:1 odds on 250 chips, but several factors suggest folding is optimal: (1) K8o is significantly dominated by Flash's likely range from button, (2) I'm out of position postflop, (3) Elon in the big blind could re-raise, putting me in an even tougher spot, (4) my stack is healthy at 18050, so I don't need to fight marginal spots, (5) better opportunities will come with premium hands where I have position or stronger equity. This is a disciplined fold that preserves chips for more favorable situations.

Flash raised to 300 from the button, which is a wide range in a 3-handed game. K8s is a strong defensive hand from the big blind with a strong king blocker, suited for playability, and good equity against a button opening range (top pair potential, straight/flush draws). Pot odds are excellent: calling 200 more into 450 (pot will be 750 after call) requires only ~27% equity, and we have much more vs. button range. Folding is too tight; 3-betting risks playing out of position with a marginal hand against a deep, aggressive opponent. Prefer to realize equity postflop in position multiway potentially, but here heads-up OOP is fine with this hand. Stack depth (54BB) supports calling.

Flop is 9h 3h Jh, a highly coordinated board with flush draw and straight draw potential (J-T, 8-10 for str8s, any hearts). Kc8s has king-high with no pair, no flush draw (offsuit, no heart), no straight draw (gutshot unlikely with this board). Pre-flop call was fine for pot odds and playability from BB vs button raise, but this flop misses completely. OOP first to act, no value to betting (bluffing into position with air is bad, especially multiway possible if Claude wakes up but he folded pre). Check-fold is optimal: realize we have ~20-30% equity vs polarized button range at best, but villain likely c-bets wide (Jx, draws, air), and we fold to bet. Pot control and avoid difficult rivers. Stack 52BB allows patience for better spots.

On a monotone flop of 9h 3h Jh, I have no hearts and very little equity with Ten-high. While I was the pre-flop aggressor, this board is quite dangerous. Elon, defending from the Big Blind, is likely to have many hands that connected with this board or contain a single heart. Checking back allows me to see a turn for free and prevents me from being check-raised off a hand that currently has some (albeit minimal) showdown value or potential to improve. If the turn is a blank and Elon checks again, I can reconsider a delayed continuation bet.

Turn pairs the 9s on a highly draw-heavy flop (flush and straight draws). We have king-high with no pair, no draw, and complete air OOP after check-check flop. Preflop call was standard realization of equity IP for villain, but we've missed entirely. Villain's flop check behind could represent weakness (missed draws/air), traps (sets/two-pair), or float. Betting as first to act is disastrous OOP: no value, poor bluff spot vs IP villain who can easily call/raise light. Checking realizes equity (free river showdown possible if villain weak), induces bluffs on scary river cards, and maintains pot control. Folding irrelevant without bet. Patience with 52BB deep; better spots await.

The turn pairs the nine, making the board 9h-3h-Jh-9c. Elon has checked to me twice. While he might be weak, the board is still very dangerous with three hearts present. I have no hearts and no real equity. Betting here as a bluff is risky against a shorter stack who might be trapping or willing to call with any Jack or heart draw. Checking back keeps the pot small and allows me to see a river for free. If Elon checks a third time on a safe river, I might consider a small bluff then, but for now, discretion is the better part of valor.

River pairs the 3s, creating a paired board with possible trips (9s or 3s), full houses (J99, 993 etc.), and completing some straight draws or spade flushes (though minimal spades before). We hold king-high with no pair, no completed draw, pure air after realizing preflop equity through check-check streets. OOP first to act on river after villain checked behind flop and turn IP on a draw-heavy board, indicating possible weakness (missed draws, floats) but also traps (monsters). Bluffing OOP into deep-stacked IP button villain is low EV: they defend wide (call light with Jx, 9x, Ax), can raise profitably, and pot odds poor for hero call-offs. Checking maximizes fold equity if villain bets thin value/bluffs, allows free showdown if villain weak, controls pot OOP, and preserves stack (52BB) for better spots in 3-handed dynamic. Patience over spew; villain's check-back history suggests showdown range without betting river.

On this board of 9h-3h-Jh-9c-3s, the best five-card hand available is two pair (Nines and Threes) with a Jack kicker (9-9-3-3-J). My hole cards (Ts 5s) do not improve this hand, as the Ten is lower than the Jack on the board. Therefore, I am 'playing the board.' I will lose to any opponent holding an Ace, King, or Queen (who would have 9-9-A-3-3, etc.), and I will split the pot with any opponent who also lacks those overcards. Elon has checked every street, suggesting he might have a weak hand or is prepared to take a showdown with a marginal high card. There is little value in bluffing here, as any Ace or King is likely to call a small bet in this spot, and I have a reasonable chance of splitting the pot by checking.

With Qd7c on the button in a 3-handed game, I need to evaluate the risk/reward. While button position is valuable in short-handed play, Qd7c is a weak hand that will frequently be dominated. Elon has a short stack (5800) and is likely to fold or push, but Flash (BB) with 16000 chips has sufficient equity to defend a wide range. The 100 chip call is small relative to my stack, but Qd7c plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position against Flash's range. I've been disciplined in recent hands and should maintain that discipline - this hand doesn't meet the threshold for hand strength needed to profitably play here. Better opportunities will come with stronger holdings.

Preflop from the small blind with K8h suited after button fold. This is a strong speculative hand with king-high, suited connectivity, and excellent playability heads-up against the big blind. Position is favorable as SB acts first but BB acts last preflop. Flash has shown a tendency to call raises frequently but also folds to aggression at times. Raising steals the dead money in the pot (150) and builds a pot with initiative. Standard size 3x BB (300 total) balances fold equity and value. My stack of 5800 (58BB) supports this without overcommitting. Folding or limping is too passive; this hand wants action.

I am in the Big Blind with 8s 2s, a very weak hand. Elon (Small Blind) has raised to 3x the big blind. Although I would have position post-flop in this 3-handed setup, 82s has poor playability and very low equity against a standard opening range. Given the strength of the other players and the marginal nature of defending such a weak hand, folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

7c4s is a complete trash hand preflop: low, offsuit, gapped, no straight or flush potential worth playing. Position on button is nice but doesn't justify entering the pot with this. Pot odds are poor (calling 100 into 150), and facing any action from SB/BB likely means strength. Deep stacks but no need to speculate with garbage. Folding preserves stack for better spots. Opponent tendencies from history show aggression, so no bluff equity here.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Eight suited (Ac 8c). After the Button (Elon) folded, it's just me and the Big Blind (Claude). Ac 8c is a very strong hand in a blind-vs-blind situation, as it has high equity and good playability. I will put in a standard open-raise to 300 to take control of the hand and build the pot against a wide range of hands that Claude might defend with.

I'm in the big blind facing a raise to 300 from Flash (small blind) after Elon folded. My hand is 6♣-10♠, which is a weak offsuit hand. While I'm getting 2:1 pot odds to call (200 to win 400), 6-10o is simply too weak to play. Against Flash's range from the small blind, this hand lacks equity and will struggle post-flop. With 17,950 chips and 98 hands remaining, there's no need to take marginal spots. It's better to fold and preserve chips for premium hands in better positions. We're only 52 hands into the tournament with plenty of opportunities ahead.

I am on the button with 9-6 offsuit. This is a very weak hand to open, even in a three-handed game. Elon in the Big Blind is the short stack with about 58 BBs and might be looking for opportunities to shove or defend aggressively. Given my recent activity level, folding this weak hand is the most disciplined play to maintain my stack.

Raising to 250 total (200 more from small blind position) to apply maximum pressure on Elon's short 5,850 chip stack. In heads-up play, Q2o has reasonable value against a short-stacked opponent who must make difficult decisions. The raise forces Elon into a choice: fold (lose time/chips) or commit heavily with whatever he's holding. My larger stack gives me a significant advantage in this leverage spot, and aggression is the correct approach when exploiting short stacks in the closing stages of a tournament.

Pocket 55 is a premium hand in BB vs SB raise after BTN fold. Claude's opening range from SB is wide (Ax+, broadway, suited connectors, small pairs) to steal blinds. 55 crushes this range with ~55% equity heads-up, set-mining value, and blocks his AA/KK. 3-betting generates fold equity (~40-50% vs wide range), builds pot when ahead, and denies equity to his floats/dominating hands. Calling risks playing OOP postflop vs c-bets; folding is -EV. Sizing to 750 (3x his 250, pot ~1100) balances value/fold equity given deep stacks but my shorter 58BB. Maximizes EV to grow stack.

Despite Elon's short stack (5200), Q2o is simply too weak to continue here. After raising to 250 preflop, Elon 3-bet me to 750, indicating strength. Q-deuce offsuit is the worst possible queen, and I'm out of position. While exploiting short stacks is important in tournament poker, it requires reasonable holdings to do so profitably. Q2o loses to the majority of Elon's reasonable 3-bet range. I've already applied pressure with my raise; folding here preserves chips for better spots where I can leverage my stack advantage more effectively. My fold also keeps me in position to steal from Flash on the button in future hands where I'll have better holdings.

I'm on the button with QJ offsuit (Queen-Jack), which is a strong broadway hand with good playability. Position is critical here - the button is the best position to steal blinds and play for profit. Elon in the small blind has a shorter stack (6,150), and Flash in the big blind has a similar stack to mine. QJ is definitely a raising hand from the button, especially against blinds. A standard 3x big blind raise (300 total) is the appropriate play here. This puts immediate pressure on both blinds, allows me to win the pot right away, and if called, I can use my position to play the hand profitably post-flop. Given my deep stack and strong position, this is textbook button play.

Qh5c is a trash hand offsuit with no connectivity or blockers. Facing a button raise from Claude, who can have a wide range, but my hand has zero equity realization OOP as SB. Pot odds are poor for calling 250 into 450 (~5.7:1 implied but hand too weak), and raising is suicidal with this holding. Standard fold to preserve stack for premium hands in this tournament setting where I'm at 61bb effective.

I am holding Ace-Queen offsuit in the Big Blind, which is a premium hand in a 3-handed game. Claude has opened from the Button, where his range is likely wide. By 3-betting, I can take control of the pot, build value, and potentially win the hand immediately. A raise to 1000 is a standard sizing to put pressure on a button opener while out of position.

Calling Flash's 1000 re-raise with QJ from the button. This hand has good equity against Flash's range, which likely includes lighter re-raises given the BB-vs-button dynamic. By calling rather than 3-betting, I preserve chips while maintaining position to play the hand profitably post-flop. QJ is too strong to fold, and calling allows me to see the flop before committing more resources. If the flop misses badly, I can fold to further aggression. My stack (17400) comfortably supports this call, and position is a significant advantage in post-flop play.

I have flopped Top Pair with a strong Queen kicker on an Ace-high board. As the pre-flop 3-better, this board favors my range significantly. I also hold the Ace of Spades, providing me with the nut flush blocker and a backdoor nut flush draw. I will place a continuation bet of approximately 35% of the pot to extract value from weaker Aces (AJ, AT, A9s), pocket pairs, and spade draws while maintaining pot control. This sizing also allows me to easily navigate the turn regardless of the card.

Calling Flash's 750 flop bet with QJ on Ts-3s-Ac. I have an open-ended straight draw (8 outs to make the nuts with any K or 9), plus high-card strength. Pot odds of 3.73:1 are favorable for my equity (~40-45% against Flash's range). Position on the button is a significant advantage for the turn and river. Stack is healthy at 16,700. This is a profitable draw play that maximizes value while maintaining control of the hand.

I hold top pair with a strong kicker on a relatively dry turn. The seven of hearts does not significantly change the board texture. Having the Ace of spades is a key blocker against the nut flush draw. Given Claude's tendency to call multiple streets (as seen in Hand #1), I will place a value bet of approximately 50% of the pot to extract chips from weaker Aces and various spade draws. This sizing also keeps the pot manageable if he has me beat with two pair (AT, A7, or A3).

Calling Flash's turn raise with QJ on Ts 3s Ac 7h. Pot odds of 3.92:1 require 25% equity; I estimate 30-35% equity with my OESD (8 straight outs) plus overcard outs. Flash's out-of-position betting suggests strength, but the range is still wide enough to justify a profitable draw call. Position is retained for the river, and my stack (15,950) comfortably supports the call. This is standard profitable draw play.

I have top pair with a strong Queen kicker. The board runout is relatively clean, with the flush draw (spades) failing to complete on the river. Claude has shown a willingness in previous hands (like Hand #1) to call down with one-pair holdings. While the nine on the river technically completes a straight for 8-6, it is an unlikely hand for Claude to call a 3-bet with pre-flop. Similarly, T-9 making two pair is possible but statistically less likely than a weak Ace. I will place a value bet of 3,100, which is approximately 43% of the pot, to extract chips from weaker Aces such as AJ, A8, or A5, while keeping the sizing small enough to potentially get called by a stubborn Ten.

On the river (9d), I have Qc Jd on board Ts 3s Ac 7h 9d. This is ace-high with Q, J, T, 9 kickers - no straight was completed, and I have no made hand strength.

Flash's action throughout this hand has been consistently aggressive: pre-flop re-raise to 1000, flop bet/raise to 750, turn raise to 1850, and now river raise to 3100. This pattern is strongly indicative of a strong made hand - likely AT (top pair), a set, two pair, or strong broadway cards like AK/AQ.

While I need only ~23% equity to call (3100 to win ~13,450), my ace-high likely has less than 20% equity against Flash's actual betting range. Flash's river raise specifically narrows their range significantly toward strong value holdings rather than bluffs, since they've had multiple opportunities to extract value throughout the hand.

Additionally, I've now committed 14,950 chips to this pot (300 pre + 700 flop + 750 flop + 1850 turn + 1850 turn). While folding forfeits this investment, calling the river with a weak hand and facing likely defeat would be compounding the error. 

This is a clear fold on a weak holding against strong opponent action.

Tc3h is a trash hand: offsuit, low cards, poor connectivity, and dominated by most calling ranges. Even on the button with position, the pot odds (150 pot, 100 to call, ~2.5:1) require about 28% equity, but this hand has far less against typical big blind completing or limping ranges from Flash and Claude. My stack is 6150 (~61BB), but preserving it for better spots is key in a 3-handed tournament phase. Opponents have massive stacks (19k+ and 14k), so no need to speculate with marginal holdings. Standard fold preflop.

I have Ace-Eight suited in the Small Blind. After the Button folded, it's just me and the Big Blind. A8s is a premium hand in this spot, well ahead of the Big Blind's random range. I will raise to 300 to build the pot and take the initiative.

Calling the 200 raise with JQ from the big blind. The hand has decent broadway potential, I'm getting 2:1 pot odds (400 chips to call 200), and my 14,000-chip stack is deep enough to see the flop. Flash has been aggressive throughout the session, but this is a standard spot to call with a reasonable hand and evaluate the flop. Out of position but with playable cards and healthy pot odds.

I raised pre-flop with Ace-Eight suited and was called by Claude in the Big Blind. The flop comes 6d-3d-6c, which is a paired board. While this board doesn't hit my range hard, it's also unlikely to have hit Claude's calling range significantly, unless he has a 6 or a pocket pair.
